figures.caption: Fig. 1: An illustrative example of a coffee-making scenario in which the prediction of human motions and actions can improve the perceived collaboration fluency to a great extent.

TABLE I: Pearson Correlation Coefficient (PCC) and Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) for all label-conditioned movement predictors over all joint angles (𝑞1, 𝑞2, 𝑞3), for D-0 and D-0-10-30. Reported values are rounded to two decimal places.
Fig. 5: Confusion matrices for D-0 (top row) and D-0-10-30 (bottom row) datasets. P denotes precision, R the recall, and F1 the Macro F1 score.

Fig. 2: Hierarchical structure of movements and actions MAHERP. Level ℎ = 0 encodes continuous movement segments, while levels ℎ ≥ 1 encode discrete actions. Edges denote temporal relations defined via Allen’s interval algebra.

Fig. 6: Configuration spaces of the first three reach movements in the sequence, namely I → C, C → A, and A → B, with before relations between consecutive movements. Black: generated motion. Red: predicted trajectories.
